unit.name: Close-ended tasks and the traps inside standard metrics
unit.description: Covers close-ended NLP tasks, standard classification metrics, benchmark aggregation, and the risk of spurious dataset artifacts.
unit.summary: Close-ended tasks are defined as tasks with a small answer space, often one or a few correct labels, so they can usually be evaluated with standard classification tools such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 [ts=535s] [ts=668s]. The lecture surveys sentiment analysis, entailment, part-of-speech tagging, named entity recognition, coreference, question answering, and multi-task benchmarks such as SuperGLUE [ts=576s] [ts=702s]. Yann then emphasizes that 'standard ML' does not mean 'easy': metric choice changes model behavior, accuracy can be misleading under class imbalance as in spam filtering, averaging heterogeneous benchmark columns is often ill-posed, and label collection can introduce spurious correlations, illustrated by SNLI hypotheses where negation alone predicts non-entailment surprisingly well [ts=858s] [ts=894s] [ts=980s].

key_points.text: A close-ended NLP task has a limited answer space, often fewer than 10 options.
Typical close-ended benchmarks include IMDb/SST for sentiment and SNLI for entailment.
Close-ended evaluation typically uses accuracy, precision, recall, and F1.
Accuracy can be deceptive on imbalanced data; a classifier predicting 'not spam' always could score 90% accuracy without solving the task.
Averaging columns that mix accuracy, F1, and correlation can yield misleading aggregate benchmark scores.
In SNLI, humans often wrote non-entailment hypotheses by inserting negation, creating a shortcut where models can do well from the hypothesis alone.

unit_summary_for_generation: Close-ended tasks are defined as tasks with a small answer space, often one or a few correct labels, so they can usually be evaluated with standard classification tools such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 [ts=535s] [ts=668s]. The lecture surveys sentiment analysis, entailment, part-of-speech tagging, named entity recognition, coreference, question answering, and multi-task benchmarks such as SuperGLUE [ts=576s] [ts=702s]. Yann then emphasizes that 'standard ML' does not mean 'easy': metric choice changes model behavior, accuracy can be misleading under class imbalance as in spam filtering, averaging heterogeneous benchmark columns is often ill-posed, and label collection can introduce spurious correlations, illustrated by SNLI hypotheses where negation alone predicts non-entailment surprisingly well [ts=858s] [ts=894s] [ts=980s].

kp_catalog.name: Close-ended NLP evaluation
kp_catalog.description: Tasks with a small answer space can often be evaluated with standard classification metrics.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because close-ended NLP evaluation formalizes how tasks with a small answer space can often be evaluated with standard classification metrics, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Metric choice and aggregation pitfalls
kp_catalog.description: Accuracy, precision, recall, F1, and aggregate leaderboard averages can mislead when class balance or metric semantics differ.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because metric choice and aggregation pitfalls clarifies accuracy, precision, recall, F1, and aggregate leaderboard averages can mislead when class balance or metric semantics differ, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Spurious correlations from dataset construction
kp_catalog.description: Benchmarks can contain shortcuts that let models exploit artifacts rather than solve the intended task.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because spurious correlations from dataset construction clarifies benchmarks can contain shortcuts that let models exploit artifacts rather than solve the intended task, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
